# How the Approach Changes Across the Stack {#sec-loop-patterns-across-stack}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"There is no single development, in either technology or management technique, which by itself promises even one order of magnitude improvement in productivity, in reliability, in simplicity."*
>
> — Fred Brooks, *No Silver Bullet* (1986)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Fred Brooks, Turing Award winner and author of *The Mythical Man-Month*, famously warned against hoping for a "silver bullet" in software engineering. His warning applies directly to AI-assisted architecture. Architecture work proceeds with very different feedback budgets, rejection checks, and decision rights, from reversible compiler tuning to silicon-facing RTL and SoC work. No single AI method fits all those conditions.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{AI-assisted architecture work spans four operating regimes distinguished by feedback latency, reversibility, decision consequence, interface span, and check independence. An AI method's role, required evidence, rejection checks, and decision rights change with those conditions. A limited service-cost model then describes cases in which review capacity, rather than candidate generation, limits progress.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
How should the AI role, evidence, checks, and decision rights change across
different parts of the computing stack?
:::

A bounded accelerator study used cheap-to-discard candidate proposals and
deterministic, replayable SCALE-Sim evaluations. The evidence supported
retaining the baseline, and the architect stopped before implementation. Those
evidence and decision rules would be inadequate if the same array geometry were
about to change an RTL interface or enter a physical-design flow. The task may
look similar while the evidence burden and decision rights change.

A compiler or kernel tuner can often compile, test, measure, and discard a bad
change in minutes. Accelerator exploration may instead rely on a simulator
whose abstractions omit the compiler path or physical effects. An ISA migration
crosses build, runtime, service, and organizational interfaces, while an RTL or
signoff decision may be costly or impossible to reverse after authorization.
These are different operating conditions even when AI proposes the change in
each case.

> **Operating regime.** An operating regime describes feedback latency,
> reversibility, decision consequence, interface span, and check independence.
> The architect uses those conditions to decide what an AI method may do, which
> evidence and checks are needed, and who may authorize the next decision. It is
> not a stack layer, a maturity score, or a property of an AI model.

Four regimes cover the cases considered here:

- **Fast and reversible:** workload packets, compilers, kernels, runtimes, and
  other work with close tests and local rollback.
- **Model-mediated architecture:** exploration of accelerator, memory,
  interconnect, chiplet, and specialized designs using feedback mainly from
  proxies, simulators, or staged tools.
- **Cross-interface and deployment:** co-design, migration, and live-system
  work whose effects cross owners or whose telemetry is noisy and confounded.
- **High consequence:** RTL, physical design, verification, signoff, and other
  work where rollback is slow, expensive, or impossible after authorization.

The same component can occupy more than one regime. A kernel schedule may be
fast and reversible during tuning, cross-interface when it changes a runtime
contract, and high consequence when hardware is frozen around it. Classify the
work by its next decision boundary, not by the noun in its title.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can classify an operating regime before deciding what work to automate.
That means you can:

- **Compare** studies by feedback, reversibility, consequence, interface span,
  and check independence;
- **Adapt** evidence and method roles without inventing a new framework for
  each stack layer;
- **Assign** who may propose, execute, reject, waive, recommend, and make the
  final decision;
- **Diagnose** when review capacity, rather than candidate supply, limits
  progress.
:::

## Classify the Regime Before Choosing the Method

Start with the next architecture decision rather than the AI method. The
architect classifies its feedback latency, rollback cost, interface span,
consequence, and check independence. Those conditions set how much work AI may
execute, what another tool or reviewer must check, and who may advance the
result.

@tbl-loop-patterns compares the four regimes. The columns describe typical
conditions, not fixed ranks. Fast feedback does not make the next decision low risk, and
silicon-facing work does not forbid useful automation. The next decision and
its consequences determine the required evidence and decision rights.

| **Operating regime** | **Feedback and reversibility** | **Interface span and check independence** | **Evidence and decision requirements** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Fast and reversible | Tests, traces, profiles, or benchmarks return quickly; a failed local change can usually be discarded or rolled back. | Usually one artifact or software boundary; automatic checks may be close to the producer. | Automate bounded generation, tuning, and repair, but test workload coverage, regressions, and the point where a local change enters a wider contract. |
| Model-mediated architecture | Proxies, simulators, and staged tools are slower or scarcer; candidates are cheap to discard before implementation. | Several architecture assumptions may interact; model and reference checks can share blind spots. | Compare against a conventional arm, test proxy mechanisms, preserve rejected regions, and require stronger evidence before implementation. |
| Cross-interface and deployment | Feedback spans layers, organizational boundaries, or live cohorts; rollback is guarded and can still impose user or operational cost. | Multiple owners and interfaces; telemetry may be noisy, confounded, private, or only partly independent. | Record interface assumptions, canaries, SLOs, rollback triggers, causal limits, and the owner of each cross-layer tradeoff. |
| High consequence | Tool feedback is slow and expensive; rollback becomes difficult toward signoff, tapeout, or irreversible deployment. | Independent flows, experts, and formal or physical checks become central; every waiver requires a recorded decision. | Use AI to narrow, critique, organize evidence, or repair bounded artifacts; reserve rejection, waivers, recommendations, and the final decision for named roles. |

: **Four operating regimes.** Feedback latency, reversibility, consequence,
interface span, and check independence determine the required evidence and
decision rights. The regimes are not a maturity ladder. {#tbl-loop-patterns tbl-colwidths="[18,25,27,22]"}

```{=latex}
\Needspace{11\baselineskip}
```

The contrast is clearest at the endpoints. A local software change can often be
executed and rolled back under close tests, while a silicon-facing change needs
independent checks, a named waiver holder, and a decision owner before it
advances. The
two middle regimes separate feedback cost from interface ownership, which is why
stack position alone cannot determine the operating regime.

The cases that follow should be read through three questions:

- **Physical constraints and rollback costs:** Which constraints apply, what
  does rollback cost, and what else can be affected if the change fails? A
  compiler flag, runtime policy, RTL edit, chiplet boundary, and fleet deployment
  do not have the same consequences.
- **Allowed actions and returned feedback:** What may the AI method change, and
  what tool, trace, log, or measurement does it receive in return?
- **Rejection checks and decision rights:** What can reject the result, where
  does required review occur, and what are the declared automation limits?

A case is credible when allowed actions, feedback, evidence, and decision rights
match the consequence of the next transition.

Check independence matters because a check can reject only the failures it can
observe. Manufacturing tests, for example, can miss silent computation errors
that fleet-scale cross-checking later exposes.

::: {.callout-field-note title="The cores that quietly did the math wrong"}
At fleet scale, Google and Meta independently found that a small number of CPU cores silently miscompute, returning wrong results with no crash and no error flag, and that these mercurial cores slip through manufacturing test and surface only across millions of machines [@HochschildEtAl2021Cores; @DixitEtAl2021SDC]. Meta traced a real corruption to missing files from one bad core, at a rate on the order of one in a thousand devices. Manufacturing test did not cover a failure that is silent by definition, so nothing rejected it until fleet scale exposed it.

A rejection check can catch only the failure classes it observes. At fleet
scale, the system needs continuous cross-checking because even the assumption
that the hardware computes correctly must be tested.
:::

The checks for any regime should therefore state which failure classes they can
observe and which require a different tool or operating context.
@tbl-loop-pattern-decision recommends a first artifact for making those limits
visible. If two rows apply, begin with the more demanding requirements until
evidence supports the narrower case.

| **Observed condition** | **Regime to start with** | **First artifact to build** | **Claim not yet supported** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Close tests and local rollback; the main uncertainty is workload coverage or code behavior. | Fast and reversible. | Versioned workload or executable test packet with coverage, failures, profiles, and rollback conditions. | A general architecture or system win. |
| Scarce simulation, many architecture knobs, or a proxy whose mechanism may fail. | Model-mediated architecture. | Action schema, matched baseline, proxy calibration, candidate record, and rejected regions. | Implementation readiness. |
| A result crosses compiler, runtime, hardware, operations, or organizational interfaces. | Cross-interface and deployment. | Interface map with versions, owners, system objectives, canary or replay plan, and rollback triggers. | A local optimum as a system result or clean causal evidence from telemetry alone. |
| The next step affects RTL, signoff, tapeout, safety, security, or another difficult-to-reverse decision. | High consequence. | Staged evidence plan with independent checks, waiver authority, residual risks, and a named decision owner. | Authority beyond the declared decision rights. |

: **A starting artifact for each regime.** Begin with the feedback conditions
and the next decision, then create the records needed to test the proposed claim.
{#tbl-loop-pattern-decision tbl-colwidths="[27,19,30,16]"}

The artifact changes with the source of uncertainty. A versioned test packet can
expose local coverage gaps. Cross-interface deployment also needs named owners
and rollback triggers, while high-consequence work needs independent checks and
explicit waiver rights and a decision owner.

### Method Roles Within Each Regime

Classifying the regime does not choose the AI method. Within any regime, AI may
predict an expensive tool result, search legal alternatives, or repair an
artifact from tool feedback. What changes is how much authority the architect
grants the method and which checks can reject its output.

- **Surrogate prediction** uses a fast proxy to triage expensive feedback and
  fails when proxy mismatch goes undetected.
- **Constrained optimization** searches legal actions under predeclared rejection
  checks. Reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-environment-contract] or another optimizer may explore the
  space, while the environment contract bounds its actions.
- **Generator-critic repair** lets a generator, such as an LLM[^fn-ml-llm-generator-critic], propose a
  bounded artifact and use compiler, simulator, formal, or synthesis failures
  to guide another attempt.

[^fn-rl-environment-contract]: In a constrained optimization loop, a reward signal can guide search only inside legal actions. The environment contract states which moves are invalid, and declared checks determine which candidates are rejected.

[^fn-ml-llm-generator-critic]: In this pattern, the LLM only proposes artifacts. Compiler, simulator, formal, or synthesis checks reject bounded artifacts, and their feedback supplies traces for the next attempt.

The sections below stay organized by operating regime because the same method
role can be useful under close tests and local rollback but inadequate when
feedback is delayed or a decision crosses an interface.

## Regime I: Fast and Reversible

This regime covers work whose state can be inspected quickly and whose failed
local changes can usually be discarded. Its limits appear when the workload is
too narrow or a local software improvement fails broader correctness and
regression tests.

### Workload and Benchmark State

Fast feedback is useful only when executable checks cover the behavior in the
claim. For workload tuning, the workload defines what behavior matters, which
metrics are meaningful, which software stack is assumed, and which design
choices can be justified. Classic workload-characterization work made this
concrete by measuring program behavior, comparing benchmark suites, and
separating inherent workload properties from artifacts of a particular machine
[@HosteEeckhout2007MICA]. Architects using AI need the same measurements in a
form that tools and reviewers can inspect. This includes traces, benchmark
versions, generated workloads, cluster assignments, coverage analysis, drift
tests, and known omissions.

An XR accelerator example shows why that detail matters. "XRBench-class
real-time mobile XR workload" is not a magic input string. XRBench provides a
benchmark anchor [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench], but the architecture question still
depends on which XR workloads, models, devices, frame-rate targets, latency
constraints, memory behaviors, and software paths are represented. An optimizer tuned to
one benchmark point may miss the distribution that a real mobile XR subsystem
must serve. The workload specification should therefore include scenario
labels, device class, trace provenance, input distributions, excluded cases,
coverage gaps, and rejection conditions.

MLPerf shows why shared benchmarks need maintained versions, rules, and
submission practices [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Workloads can change quickly in active ML-serving
domains, for example when dense Transformers give way to
Mixture-of-Experts[^fn-ml-moe-workload-drift]. The workload packet should
therefore record the benchmark version, source, coverage claims, known gaps,
leakage risks, and the conditions under which a result should not generalize.
It also needs a defined process for adding new computation graphs, traces, and
coverage tests.

[^fn-ml-moe-workload-drift]: The MoE point here is workload drift. A model-family shift can change communication, memory, and scheduling pressure fast enough that a static benchmark needs a defined refresh path.

@tbl-workload-characterization-20 shows what must be added when an automated
optimizer acts on workload data. Traditional profiling, benchmark construction,
and performance comparison remain necessary. The additional records identify
the workload version, coverage, provenance, and checks that can reject a result
based on a stale, narrow, or leaky slice.

| **Established task** | **AI-assisted extension** | **Required record or state** | **Failure if missing** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Select representative workloads or benchmark suites. | Define the versioned workload distribution the optimizer may use. | Scenario metadata, input distributions, versions, provenance, and inclusion/exclusion rationale. | The optimizer overfits one stale or convenient slice and reports a false win. |
| Profile behavior: locality, branch behavior, memory traffic, bandwidth, latency, energy, and phase behavior. | Record workload features for prediction, search, critique, and active test selection. | Feature schema, measurement provenance, tool configuration, uncertainty, and known blind spots. | A predictor learns a proxy that does not survive another phase, input, or fidelity level. |
| Compare architectures under a fixed benchmark. | Maintain an evidence record across workload variants, candidate designs, and fidelity levels. | Candidate IDs, workload IDs, simulator/tool versions, feedback cost, accepted results, and rejected results. | Design-space results cannot be audited or reused in another study. |
| Build or curate benchmarks for community comparison. | Define an environment contract: valid tasks, inputs, actions, metrics, leakage rules, and rejection checks. | Benchmark harness, validity checks, metric definitions, seeds, test splits, and update policy. | The optimizer overfits benchmark artifacts or proposes actions outside the intended task. |
| Explain why a workload matters. | Record workload intent, deployment context, and drift so the named owner can accept or reject a claim. | Use-case assumptions, deployment constraints, quality-of-service targets, telemetry hooks, and review notes. | The AI method produces a plausible result for the wrong product or deployment regime. |
| Summarize results for a paper. | Preserve workload evidence as reusable architecture data. | Evidence record, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and rationale for final claims. | Architects repeat invalid experiments or lose why prior choices were rejected. |

: **Workload records for an AI-assisted study.** Profiling and benchmark construction remain necessary, but the workload record must also include versions, provenance, coverage, rejection conditions, and failed runs. {#tbl-workload-characterization-20 tbl-colwidths="[20,26,25,20]"}

AI methods can help cluster traces, summarize behavior, generate missing cases,
and identify coverage gaps. Their output is useful only when the benchmark
harness retains versioned traces, metadata, update rules, leakage checks, and
drift tests. Those records let reviewers decide whether a design result can
reasonably be applied beyond the measured slice.

### Fast Software Feedback

Compiler flags, kernels, library implementations, runtime policies,
configuration settings, and small code repairs often fit the fast-and-reversible
regime because failed changes can usually be evaluated quickly and rolled back.
Feedback may come from unit tests, microbenchmarks, integration tests, profilers,
telemetry, or canary deployment[^fn-systems-canary-deployment].

[^fn-systems-canary-deployment]: A canary deployment is a controlled, partial rollout that exposes a change to a small slice of traffic to catch regressions before a system-wide release. It is one of the cheap, reversible checks that let an optimizer or repair method iterate quickly because a bad change can be caught and rolled back before a wider rollout is authorized.

Close feedback lets an optimizer search more aggressively. Autotuning
systems and learned tensor-program optimizers[^fn-ml-tensor-program-optimizers] show how search spaces, cost
models, measurements, and scheduling can be combined to improve software
performance across targets
[@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor]. That combination is useful for
AI-assisted architecture when generation is limited to artifacts with
executable checks and cheap rollback.

[^fn-ml-tensor-program-optimizers]: Tensor-program optimizers automate the search for efficient execution schedules of tensor operations on a target, using cost models and on-device measurements to steer the search. Their cheap feedback supports aggressive automated search. The same permission does not extend to a hardware-interface decision, which is harder to reverse.

Kernel-generation benchmarks make the same point in a current form.
KernelBench evaluates whether models can produce GPU kernels that are both
correct and faster than a baseline [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench]. This work is
fast and reversible because correctness tests, compilation, profiling, and
microbenchmark feedback are close to the generated artifact. It also touches
hardware/software co-design because performance depends on memory layout,
parallelism, numerical precision, backend behavior, and target-specific
hardware resources. Multi-platform kernel-generation work exposes this
cross-layer dependency by separating the core benchmark from target backends
such as Triton, a Python-embedded GPU-kernel DSL, and CUDA
[@WenEtAl2025MultiKernelBench].
A kernel-generation benchmark needs more than a prompt and a score. It should
record kernel IDs, input-shape distributions, correctness oracles, target
metadata, failed compiles and profiles, and rejected variants.

For bounded code generation or tuning, architects preserve the source code,
compiler IR, tests, target metadata, profiles, and exact compiler and runtime
versions.
Compilation, tests, and profiles return quickly on the artifact. A generated
kernel can be tested, a compiler flag reverted, and a runtime policy tried in a
canary. This proximity permits more automated iteration, while correctness,
compilation, portability, regressions, and maintainability can still reject a
candidate. A named owner must approve a local kernel change before it enters a
wider software or hardware interface.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="Fast feedback can miss system failures"}
An AI-generated kernel that wins on one input size may regress another. An
AI-proposed runtime policy that improves average latency may worsen tail
latency. Even with fast feedback, architects must record workload coverage,
who may reject a candidate, and who owns the decision before the change enters
a larger system.
:::

## Regime II: Model-Mediated Architecture

In architecture design-space exploration, candidate parameters are cheap to
change, but trustworthy feedback may require scarce simulations or staged tool
runs. The search may vary accelerator organization, vector width, cache
hierarchy, local memory, interconnect, chiplet partitioning, and package
assumptions. It may also choose how work is divided across CPUs, accelerators,
and SoC blocks.
Feedback is slower than software tests and less definitive than silicon.
Actions can violate design constraints, and proxies can fail outside the region
where they were calibrated. The space is too large for exhaustive enumeration.

Model-mediated exploration requires an explicit representation of architecture
state, legal actions, and tool observations. Methods may generate candidates,
predict behavior, allocate evaluations, or critique assumptions, while the run
record retains failed runs and rejected alternatives. ArchGym provides one such
interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym], and predictive design-space exploration
has long used data-driven models for similar purposes
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. Apollo extends this idea across accelerator design
spaces by training a surrogate on an architecture dataset
[@yazdanbakhsh2021apollo]. The credibility of its predictions depends on the
dataset's schema, sampled-region coverage, tool versions, invalid-action labels,
rejected regions, and proxy calibration.

Chiplet exploration adds partitioning and die-to-die interfaces to the available
design choices. For a UCIe-class interface, the architect should specify what
may be split across dies, the bandwidth, latency, power, and thermal
measurements returned by the tools, the fixed package and software assumptions,
and the evidence required before a candidate
can advance [@UCIeConsortium2026Spec].

A completed array study illustrates the gap between simulator evidence and
implementation evidence. SCALE-Sim could compare legal array geometries under
one frozen workload packet, but that evidence did not establish a compiler
path, memory-system behavior, RTL correctness, or physical feasibility. The
results supported retaining the baseline for the simulated workload and
metrics; authorizing implementation remains separate.
Promoting the same candidate toward implementation would put the next decision
in a more demanding regime and require new evidence.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Cross-Layer Rejection Check"}
When an optimizer tunes against a local proxy such as core area, check whether
the harness includes checks for candidates that win only by pushing complexity
into memory traffic, compiler support, verification, or SoC integration.
:::

In this regime, AI may generate candidates, predict tool results, search the
legal space, or critique assumptions. The method may rank candidates under the
declared simulator or proxy, but implementation remains a separate decision.
Illegal actions, simulator mismatch, power or software incompatibility, and
weak Pareto evidence can stop a candidate. The architect decides whether
stronger feedback warrants moving it toward implementation.

### Specialization and Executable Software Paths

Specialization can improve efficiency, but a domain is not one knob. It may be
a kernel or model family, data type, memory pattern, programming model,
deployment regime, latency envelope, product vertical, or software ecosystem.
The golden-age argument for specialization [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]
therefore requires architects to establish which properties are stable enough
to specialize and which must remain programmable.

Before a local speedup can support a system claim, the architect must account for
the interface and software path. Amdahl's law
[@Amdahl1967ValiditySingleProcessor], its multicore reinterpretation
[@HillMarty2008AmdahlMulticore], and the accelerator-focused LogCA model
[@AltafWood2017LogCA] all prevent a local speedup from standing in for an
end-to-end result. A compact form that includes interface and software cost
is
$$
S_{\mathrm{system}} \le
\frac{1}{(1-f) + f/s + \epsilon_{\mathrm{interface}} +
\epsilon_{\mathrm{software}}}.
$$
Here, $f$ is the improvable fraction of baseline time, $s$ is local speedup,
and the $\epsilon$ terms are interface and software overheads normalized to the
same baseline. The specialization helps only when each invocation does enough
work to amortize those overheads. The resulting report should therefore include
the interface, software path, and end-to-end measurement rather than only the
local speedup. Review capacity can impose a separate bottleneck when candidate
production outpaces the trusted checks needed to settle those results.

A simplified LogCA-style calculation in @fig-logca-breakeven quantifies the
interface tax [@AltafWood2017LogCA]. End-to-end speedup appears only after work per
offload clears a break-even point set by latency and overhead.

```{python}
#| label: fig-logca-breakeven
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Offload overhead determines the break-even granularity.** In a simplified LogCA-style model [@AltafWood2017LogCA], end-to-end speedup appears only after each offload performs enough work to amortize latency and overhead, then approaches the accelerator's peak acceleration. A more distant accelerator needs more work per offload to break even. The curves are illustrative; the argument depends on this behavior rather than the exact values.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Line plot showing offload speedup curves that rise only after enough work is amortized to overcome interface latency and overhead."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

A = 100.0  # peak acceleration (asymptote)
g = [10 ** (6 * i / 399) for i in range(400)]

curves = [
    {"label": "tightly coupled accelerator", "ovh": 2.0, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"label": "shared-memory SoC block", "ovh": 40.0, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"label": "off-chip / discrete module", "ovh": 1500.0, "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

def speedup(gran, ovh):
    # LogCA: Speedup(g) = C*g / (o + L + C*g/A), with C = 1, beta = 1, (o + L) = ovh
    return gran / (ovh + gran / A)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.25, 3.12))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.86, top=0.82, bottom=0.28)

ax.axhline(A, color=COLORS["ink"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(1.35, A * 1.08, "peak acceleration A", ha="left", va="bottom", fontsize=6.2, color=COLORS["ink"])
ax.axhline(1.0, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=1)
ax.text(7.5e5, 1.10, "break-even", ha="right", va="bottom", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["muted"])

ax.axhspan(0.25, 1.0, color=COLORS["red"], alpha=0.08, zorder=0)
ax.text(1.5, 0.38, "offload loses", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["red"], fontstyle="italic")

for c in curves:
    s = [speedup(x, c["ovh"]) for x in g]
    ax.plot(g, s, color=c["color"], linewidth=1.9, zorder=3, label=c["label"])
    g1 = c["ovh"] / (1.0 - 1.0 / A)
    ax.scatter([g1], [1.0], marker="o", s=22, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=c["color"], linewidth=1.3, zorder=4, clip_on=False)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1, 1e6)
ax.set_ylim(0.25, 150)
ax.set_xlabel("operation granularity  (work amortized per offload)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("end-to-end speedup", fontsize=7)
ax.set_yticks([0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"0.3$\times$", r"1$\times$", r"3$\times$", r"10$\times$", r"30$\times$", r"100$\times$"], fontsize=6.4)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2, labelsize=6.4)
ax.grid(axis="both", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=0)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

legend = fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=3, fontsize=6.0, frameon=False,
                    bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.4)
for text in legend.get_texts():
    text.set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Simplified LogCA-style view: the farther the accelerator sits from the host, the larger the granularity needed before offload breaks even.",
    xywh=(0.10, 0.03, 0.80, 0.11),
    fontsize=5.5,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The curves show that greater coupling distance increases interface overhead and
raises the amount of work needed to break even. The tightly coupled accelerator
clears break-even after modest work per offload, while the off-chip module needs
orders of magnitude more before offload wins at all. A specialization proposal
that cannot state its expected work per invocation therefore cannot claim an
end-to-end speedup.

A benchmark name does not establish a stable domain.
@fig-domain-specificity-shapes asks what must remain stable before an architect
can justify specialization. Its six surrounding groups cover the kernel family,
data and precision, memory behavior, program interface, deployment conditions,
and drift and verification.

![**Properties that define a specialization domain.** A domain-specific architecture specializes for a combination of kernel, precision, data-movement, interface, deployment, drift, and verification requirements, not for an application label alone.](images/F10a-domain-specificity-shapes){#fig-domain-specificity-shapes width="100%" fig-alt="Multi-shape diagram showing that a domain-specific architecture study must characterize kernels, precision, data movement, interfaces, deployment constraints, drift, and verification needs before claiming specialization."}

The upper groups in @fig-domain-specificity-shapes describe the computation and
data behavior the hardware will exploit. The lower groups describe the
software interface, deployment envelope, and evidence needed to keep the claim
valid as workloads and tools change. The specialization claim is only as broad
as the combination of those conditions. Once they are clear, the software path
determines whether the workload can use the specialized hardware. Halide-style
algorithm/schedule separation, AutoTVM/Ansor-style measured schedules, and
MLIR-style representations make the mapping choices explicit and allow
compiler checks to reject invalid paths
[@RaganKelleyEtAl2017HalideCACM; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor;
@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. Programming models,
compiler IRs, libraries, runtimes, and generated code connect domain intent to
hardware or deployment evidence (@fig-codegen-narrow-waist).

![**The software path to specialized hardware.** Domain-specific architecture claims require executable software paths and evidence about correctness, performance, portability, and maintenance.](images/F10b-codegen-narrow-waist){#fig-codegen-narrow-waist width="92%" fig-alt="Narrow-waist diagram showing programming models, compiler representations, libraries, runtimes, and generated code connecting specialized hardware to evidence checks."}

In @fig-codegen-narrow-waist, domain intent enters from the top, while
architecture mechanisms and hardware constraints shape what can be built. The
programming model, compiler IR, libraries, runtime, and generated code form the
executable path that makes the specialized hardware usable. Tests, profiling,
simulation, synthesis, and telemetry then return results about correctness,
performance, portability, and maintenance. Reviewers use those results to test
the architecture claim. A local kernel result cannot bypass this software path
and still support that claim.

Generative methods can help produce kernels and schedules because
kernel-generation benchmarks keep correctness, compilation, profiling, and
target behavior close to the artifact [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench;
@WenEtAl2025MultiKernelBench]. An architecture claim also needs end-to-end
software and hardware evidence. The design-loop card records the domain requirements,
kernel family, data layout, compiler IR, runtime interface, backend capabilities,
tests, portability limits, and maintenance evidence. Its feedback budget is
split. Declared automation may tune cheap, reversible kernel and schedule
choices, while interface decisions require end-to-end evidence and a named
decision owner. Correctness, portability, interface cost, workload drift, and
system measurement can reject the local win.

That software path also defines where automated tuning must stop and a hardware
interface decision begins.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Hardware-Interface Authorization Boundary"}
Record which kernel and schedule actions automation may execute or recommend, which hardware
interface changes require stronger evidence, and who is authorized to approve
them. A local kernel improvement does not by itself justify an interface
change.
:::

Without a declared hardware-interface authorization boundary, a local benchmark
win can look like an architectural result even when the compiler and the
complete system cannot use it.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="The accelerator that won in isolation"}
An AI-generated block posts a large kernel speedup, but the compiler cannot keep
it busy, data movement dominates, and only hand-written code reaches the
headline result. The evaluation measured isolated kernel speedup instead of
end-to-end behavior. Include the interface and software path, then require a
compiler-generated end-to-end measurement before promoting the candidate.
:::

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

Keeping a local kernel win from bypassing compiler and system checks requires
the run record to carry the software mapping through each stage of lowering.
Some domain-specific or reconfigurable searches therefore include the compiler
in represented design state. An AI method may
propose a custom compute unit, IR dialects, and lowering rules within a specified
software contract. MLIR and CIRCT (Circuit IR Compilers and Tools)
verifiers and legality checks,
simulation, equivalence tests, and synthesis can reject stages of that path.
Passing an early verifier does not establish semantic or physical validity.
The run record must preserve the hardware and software pair sketched
schematically in @lst-mlir-co-generation.

```{.llvm #lst-mlir-co-generation lst-cap="**Schematic co-generated hardware and software IR:** Pseudo-MLIR showing the loop record, not executable CIRCT/MLIR."}
// Schematic Architecture 2.0 loop record: co-generated HW and SW.
// The loop proposes a hardware dialect and lowering strategy for evaluation.

// 1. The AI defines the hardware intent using CIRCT (Hardware IR)
hw.module @CustomXR_SystolicArray(in %clk: i1, in %act: tensor<16x16xf16>, out %res: tensor<16x16xf16>) {
  // Agent-generated spatial architecture constraints
  %area_est = hw.predict_area(...)
  // Loop rejects if %area_est > mobile_XR_budget
  // Note: Area is heavily approximated here; true IR drop or routing congestion requires physical synthesis.
}

// 2. The AI simultaneously generates the compiler scheduling policy
// using MLIR Transform Dialect to map the XR workload to the new hardware
transform.sequence failures(propagate) {
^bb1(%arg1: !pdl.operation):
  // Agent discovers tiling to 16x16 is optimal for the power envelope
  %0, %loops:2 = transform.structured.tile %arg1 [16, 16]

  // Agent generates the mapping rule to the custom hardware it just designed
  transform.structured.map_to_custom_hw %0 { target = @CustomXR_SystolicArray }
}
```

The record keeps the proposed hardware and its scheduling policy together. A
failure in either part rejects the pair. The predicted-area check can screen
against the mobile XR budget, but physical synthesis must replace that early
estimate before the design advances.

## Regime III: Cross-Interface and Deployment

### Cross-Layer Co-Design

Cross-layer co-design examines interactions among software behavior, hardware
mechanisms, physical constraints, and system objectives. A single-layer
optimization can therefore be locally correct and still make the complete
system worse.

Consider a dataflow choice that reduces compute cycles but increases memory
traffic, a topology change that improves one collective while worsening
another, a cache change that improves average performance but increases tail
latency, or a rack-level policy that saves power while violating service
quality. The represented state must include more than one layer because the
objective spans them.

Data movement, memory locality, network contention, power and thermal headroom,
and deployment policy can all invalidate a local compute improvement before
expensive implementation evidence is gathered
[@Horowitz2014Energy; @BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].

A co-design representation should therefore include workload phases, data movement,
memory locality, network behavior, power and thermal constraints, compiler and
runtime choices, and deployment policy. It should state which layer an
automated optimizer may change, where independent feedback comes from, which
objective improved or worsened, and who may approve the tradeoff.

The first-generation TPU provides a concrete example. It was designed with the
software stack that quantized models for the device, and its deployed serving
objective included a 99th-percentile latency bound
[@JouppiEtAl2017TPU]. The 8-bit datapath was therefore a cross-layer decision,
and a throughput improvement that violated the latency target was not
acceptable.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Cross-Layer Ownership"}
Before an optimizer acts, record which layer it may change, which other layer
supplies independent feedback, what automation may execute or recommend, and
who may approve the cross-layer tradeoff.
:::

At warehouse scale, architects must consider workloads, software, hardware,
networking, power, cooling, and operations together. Models, traces, and
deployment measurements may all contribute evidence. A local improvement
cannot advance when it violates a system objective, and cross-layer changes
require approval from the responsible owners.

In early accelerator, compiler, runtime, or system-interface work, the hardware
target and the software path may both be changing. The architect must evaluate
them together instead of searching a fixed space. A new
instruction, tensor unit, scratchpad policy, data layout, or runtime interface
changes what software can express, while compiler, kernel, and workload
feedback changes which hardware feature is worth keeping.

Software feedback supports an architecture claim only when the run record ties
each code path to the hardware snapshot it targeted and the compiler rules that
produced it, the checks and measurements returned, the failed runs and variants
rejected, and the owner who reviewed the result.
@fig-hardware-software-coevolution shows those two changing histories joined by
one claim.

![**Recording hardware and software changes together.** Early architecture work often changes the hardware target and software path at the same time. Both sides should be versioned with their artifacts, tests, measurements, rejected variants, architecture claim, and decision review.](images/F10c-hardware-software-coevolution){#fig-hardware-software-coevolution width="100%" fig-alt="Two-lane diagram showing hardware target state and software path state evolving together through a shared study record and review of an architectural claim by a named decision holder."}

The left lane in @fig-hardware-software-coevolution versions the target
specification, hardware snapshot, and implementation hooks. The right lane
versions the software interface, generated path, and workload feedback.
Changes in either lane can alter the other, so both feed one run record and one
review by the decision holder. Without that link, a strong generated kernel may
prove only that one artifact matched one snapshot, not that the architecture
improved. A rejection on either side must invalidate the paired claim or
trigger a new evaluation against the revised hardware and software state.

### Deployment Feedback and Migration

Deployment-facing work includes scheduling, serving, admission control,
memory allocation, power management, placement, rollout policy, fleet
telemetry, and performance isolation in real or representative systems. Their
feedback can be more relevant than simulation, but it is also noisy,
confounded, private, and difficult to reproduce. Evaluation therefore needs
scenarios, latency and power limits, cohort provenance, canary labels, privacy
filters, drift tests, rollback triggers, and rollback outcomes. The report
should distinguish a signal that supports a causal explanation from one that
only warns that earlier evidence may no longer apply. Production telemetry can
guide the next bounded study, but rarely proves a design claim on its own.

Feedback also arrives at different times. Tool results may return during
development, fleet telemetry after a rollout, and software improvements months
or years after the hardware ships. Compilers, kernel libraries, and runtimes
often recover performance that launch-day evaluation could not show.
Resubmissions on unchanged hardware across MLPerf rounds demonstrate that this
can happen [@ReddiEtAl2020MLPerfInference], but they do not establish a reliable
trajectory for every platform. Baseline comparisons should therefore state the
maturity of both software stacks. Any projected improvement should be based on
platform-specific history, explicit assumptions, uncertainty, and a future
observation that would disprove the projection.

A measured example is Google's warehouse-scale migration from x86 to Arm
[@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. This is a software migration rather than a
hardware co-design case. The target ISA is fixed, and the work adapts software
so production services can move to it. The reported effort analyzed
more than 38,000 migration commits. An AI-assisted repair tool was evaluated
separately on a selected replay benchmark of 245 historical commits with
identifiable build and test targets. It produced a passing repair for 30 percent
of that benchmark, a directional result rather than a live fleet automation
rate [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. The paper reports no matched
conventional repair baseline, so the result does not by itself measure how much
AI assistance improved the repair process.

Builds, tests, sanitizers for memory-model differences, and production monitors
can reject regressions, crash loops, or slow jobs. Cases that these checks
cannot resolve go to the application owner. @tbl-isa-migration-loop-card records
the migration state, checks, failed cases, and owners without depending on a
particular model.

| **Card field** | **Migration reading** |
| --- | --- |
| **Task** | Move production services across an ISA boundary while preserving build, test, and rollout behavior. |
| **Representation** | Migration commits, build targets, tests, sanitizer findings, service ownership, and evicted production cases. |
| **Environment** | Build-and-test repair loop plus production monitoring on the new target. |
| **Method role** | Propose bounded software repairs and route failed cases toward owners. |
| **Rejection checks and authority** | Build failures, test failures, sanitizer failures, crash loops, slow jobs, and monitor-triggered evictions. |
| **Accountable decision** | Automated repair can clear only the cases its declared checks settle; application owners receive the exceptions. |

: **Records for an ISA migration.** The migration record requires represented state,
independent checks, preserved failed cases, and application-owned exceptions.
{#tbl-isa-migration-loop-card tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

The most instructive result is what the evidence overturned. The paper describes
a conventional expectation that low-level code translation would dominate an
ISA migration. @fig-isa-migration-effort compares that expectation with the
recorded distribution of commits and changed lines across code adaptation,
testing, build and deployment, and supporting tools.

```{python}
#| label: fig-isa-migration-effort
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Where the migration effort actually went:** Across the 38,156 recorded commits of Google's warehouse-scale x86-to-Arm migration, the paper's Code Adaptation & Correction group, including intrinsics, concurrency, and data-representation fixes, accounts for about 1 percent of commits and 3.5 percent of changed lines. Build, Deployment & Infrastructure Configuration dominates commit counts, and Supporting Processes & Tools, including migration tooling, monitoring, and rollbacks, dominates changed lines [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. The paper describes the conventional prior qualitatively, so only the recorded distribution is charted.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Grouped horizontal bar chart of recorded effort in Google's x86-to-Arm migration across five commit-category groups, showing share of commits and share of changed lines of code. Code adaptation and correction, the group expected to dominate, holds about one percent of commits and three and a half percent of changed lines, while build, deployment, and infrastructure configuration holds about 87 percent of commits and supporting processes and tools hold about 62 percent of changed lines."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter9-isa-migration-effort.csv
# Christopher et al., "Instruction Set Migration at Warehouse Scale"
# (arXiv:2510.14928), Figure 1 [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. Group-level
# commit and changed-LoC counts are sums of the paper's published per-category
# values (38,156 commits / 734,621 changed LoC in total; sums verified against
# the stated totals). Percentages are derived here as count/total*100. The
# paper states the expectation that low-level code translation would dominate
# only qualitatively (Sec 1, 4.1); no numeric expected shares exist, so only
# the recorded distribution is charted.
groups = [
    # (label, commits, changed LoC)
    ("Code adaptation\n& correction", 378, 25392),
    ("Test adaptation\n& configuration", 1579, 50835),
    ("Build, deployment &\ninfra configuration", 33342, 198351),
    ("Supporting processes\n& tools", 2840, 458715),
    ("Uncategorized", 17, 1328),
]
TOTAL_COMMITS = 38156
TOTAL_LOC = 734621

pct_commits = [100.0 * g[1] / TOTAL_COMMITS for g in groups]
pct_loc = [100.0 * g[2] / TOTAL_LOC for g in groups]


def bar_label(pct):
    return f"{pct:.1f}%" if pct >= 0.1 else "<0.1%"


fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.25, 2.95))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.235, right=0.965, top=0.87, bottom=0.30)

ypos = list(range(len(groups)))
height = 0.34
ax.barh([y - 0.19 for y in ypos], pct_commits, height=height,
        color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=2, label="share of commits")
ax.barh([y + 0.19 for y in ypos], pct_loc, height=height,
        color=COLORS["orange"], zorder=2, label="share of changed lines")

for y, pc, pl in zip(ypos, pct_commits, pct_loc):
    ax.annotate(bar_label(pc), (pc, y - 0.19), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(3, 0), va="center", fontsize=5.8, color=COLORS["ink"])
    ax.annotate(bar_label(pl), (pl, y + 0.19), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(3, 0), va="center", fontsize=5.8, color=COLORS["ink"])

ax.annotate("expected to dominate", (14, 0),
            fontsize=6.0, va="center", color=COLORS["constraints_ink"],
            fontstyle="italic")

ax.set_yticks(ypos)
ax.set_yticklabels([g[0] for g in groups], fontsize=6.2)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlim(0, 100)
ax.set_xticks([0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100])
ax.set_xlabel("share of recorded migration effort (%)", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2, labelsize=6.4)
ax.grid(axis="x", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55, zorder=0)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

legend = fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=2, fontsize=6.0, frameon=False,
                    bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.4)
for text in legend.get_texts():
    text.set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "38,156 commits, ~700K changed lines. The source states the expectation that code translation would dominate only qualitatively, so the chart shows the recorded distribution alone.",
    xywh=(0.10, 0.03, 0.80, 0.11),
    fontsize=5.5,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The blue bars in @fig-isa-migration-effort show that build, deployment, and
infrastructure configuration account for about 87 percent of commits. The
orange bars show that supporting processes and tools, including migration
tooling, monitoring, and rollback, account for about 62 percent of changed
lines. Code adaptation and correction account for only about 1 percent of
commits and 3.5 percent of changed lines. Most recorded effort went to the
interfaces and supporting systems around the code, not to low-level
translation. That finding overturns a plausible prior without treating the
prior as universal among application owners.

The migration record also shows what evidence an ISA proposal ultimately
needs.
Arm and RISC-V are different targets, but both make generated code, ABI and
memory-model assumptions, compiler and runtime behavior, libraries, tests, and
rollout part of the architecture evidence. An automated proposal is credible
only when the run record shows which software survives that contract and which
cases reach their application owner.

For deployment-facing work, the card should include operational state, workload
drift, SLOs[^fn-systems-slo], resource contention, user
constraints, and rollback mechanisms. Evaluation may use a simulator, test
cluster, replay harness, staging system, or guarded production system. Automated
actions should remain narrow, such as adjusting a scheduler weight, proposing a
canary, changing an admission threshold, or selecting a replay cohort. Each
action should identify its telemetry, rollback trigger, automation limit, and
owner.

[^fn-systems-slo]: In a deployment-facing loop, SLOs provide operational rejection conditions. A proposed policy change that would breach a target can be stopped before it commits [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE].

Operational checks include SLOs, canary guards, alerts,
safety policies, privacy constraints, and review by the responsible operator.
For datacenter-scale claims, network congestion and tail latency may matter as
much as average throughput or node-level power. Rollback reduces risk but does
not eliminate it; a poor policy can waste power, violate latency targets,
create interference, or harm users before it is withdrawn. The mercurial-core
case described earlier shows a more difficult failure, a silent error that
manufacturing tests missed and fleet-scale cross-checking eventually found.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Operational Stop Path"}
Reversibility is not free. Every proposed policy change needs a declared
automation limit, an operational rejection check such as a canary guard or SLO
monitor, and a named owner who can stop the rollout before it wastes
power or damages user experience.
:::

A design that looks strong under a static benchmark may face different workload
mixes, model versions, traffic patterns, or fleet policies after deployment.
Production evidence is therefore necessary, but telemetry should not be treated
as a clean causal measurement.

## Regime IV: High Consequence

High-consequence work includes RTL changes, generator-level edits, physical
design, timing closure, layout, power analysis (including IR drop and thermal
integrity), Design Rule Checking (DRC), formal verification, signoff, and other
silicon-facing decisions. Tool flows are complex, feedback is delayed, and an
error can require another implementation cycle or survive into silicon.
Evidence must survive independent checks, and a named role must authorize the
next transition.

In this regime, a candidate stops being only a plausible design-space result
and starts making claims that must survive RTL checks, physical constraints,
power analysis, verification, and integration review.

AI assistance is most useful here in critique, search narrowing, evidence
organization, bounded repair, test generation, report summarization, and
inconsistency detection. Finding a missing assumption, organizing tool results,
explaining why a candidate failed, or narrowing a physical-design search space
can remove real bottlenecks while named reviewers retain final authority.

Learning-assisted chip placement is a prominent example of a design subflow
being formulated as a learning problem [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].
Even when learning methods help with physical design, a placement result still
needs tool constraints, baselines, provenance, rejection authority, and
assigned decision rights.

Public reports show that learning methods can contribute in this regime.
Vendor-reported reinforcement-learning systems have
searched the physical-implementation flow for commercial tapeouts at scale
[@Synopsys2023DSOai], while peer-reviewed PrefixRL work reports synthesis
results for parallel-prefix circuits in an industrial process technology
[@RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL].
The evidence differs across these reports. PrefixRL reports peer-reviewed
synthesis evidence, while the cited DSO.ai source is a vendor report. Reviewing
the AlphaChip evidence requires the same separation of public claims, private
evidence, proxy results, and independent physical checks. Reviewers should judge
each claim according to its evidence and independent checks. Cost also matters.
A search that consumes many machine-hours per block is easier to justify when
its result will be instantiated across many shipped units. The record should
include the initial design state, allowed tool settings, tool versions, failed
candidates, signoff checks, waivers, escalation points, and the person
authorized to make the final decision.

Post-silicon bring-up and validation failures belong in that same record. They
provide direct evidence about realized behavior. Architects should use them to
update future design-loop cards, retire stale assumptions, and record the
conditions under which earlier proxy or tool evidence stopped applying.

Parsers, type checks, regression suites, formal tools, synthesis, timing, power
analysis, layout rules, signoff flows, integration review, and expert judgment
can all reject a candidate. When one fails, the architect must revise the
representation, action space, method, or claim before continuing.

The public AlphaChip disagreement provides a neutral test of what
high-consequence review must align before independent groups can compare results.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Strong checks still produced incompatible conclusions"}
**The claim.** The original AlphaChip authors and later independent groups used synthesis, timing,
power, placement-rule, and expert review within their own flows
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL].

**The gap.** The cases, baselines, budgets, implementations, and tool flows did not
fully align. A result rejected in one flow therefore did not necessarily test
the same claim as a result accepted in another
[@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** At this level of consequence, strong private checks are not enough to settle a
public comparison. The parties also need common cases and disclosed evaluation
conditions, or a precise account of why the conditions differ. Independent
review cannot resolve a disagreement when it is reviewing a different object.
:::

For bounded RTL, generator, or physical-design work, the card records
implementation state, constraints, tests, tool reports, waivers, and review
decisions. AI may help narrow the search, critique a result, repair a bounded
artifact, organize evidence, or generate tests. Independent tools and experts
retain authority to reject the result, and the decision record names the owner
and automation limit.

## When Review Capacity Becomes the Bottleneck {#sec-the-rejection-bound}

The four regimes can encounter the same practical limit. Architects may assume
that a slow architecture search needs more candidates even when tools and
reviewers can settle only a few of the candidates already arriving. Just as a
kernel can be memory-bound rather than compute-bound
[@WilliamsWatermanPatterson2009Roofline],
review can become the bottleneck. If candidate proposals arrive at rate
$\lambda_{\mathrm{propose}}$ while the trusted review path can settle them at
rate $\mu_{\mathrm{review}}$, review is the bottleneck when
$\lambda_{\mathrm{propose}} > \mu_{\mathrm{review}}$. Candidate supply then
builds a queue instead of increasing the number of reviewed decisions.

An Amdahl-like cost model describes one restricted setting, a fixed stream of
comparable candidates
[@Amdahl1967ValiditySingleProcessor; @AltafWood2017LogCA]. Let
$c_{\mathrm{hi}}$ be the mean service cost when every item goes directly to the
high-fidelity reference, and let $c_{\mathrm{cheap}} < c_{\mathrm{hi}}$ be the
mean cost of the cheaper check. Let $f$ be the fraction correctly settled without
high-fidelity escalation, and let $a$ be the fraction that incurs the cheap
check. Here, $f$ plays the same mathematical role as the improvable fraction in
the specialization bound, but for review cost rather than execution time.
Holding the candidate distribution, reference decisions, and review concurrency
fixed, the service-cost speedup is
$$
S_{\mathrm{review}} =
\frac{1}{(1-f) + a \cdot (c_{\mathrm{cheap}}/c_{\mathrm{hi}}) + \epsilon}
\le
\frac{1}{(1-f) + f \cdot (c_{\mathrm{cheap}}/c_{\mathrm{hi}}) + \epsilon}.
$$
The inequality follows because $f \le a \le 1$. Screening every candidate gives
$a=1$; the optimistic upper bound assumes perfect routing, so the cheap check is
used only for cases it settles and $a=f$. The overhead
$\epsilon \ge 0$, distinct from the interface and software overheads of the
specialization bound, covers routing, queueing, audit, and rework beyond the high-fidelity
service already charged by $(1-f)$, normalized by $c_{\mathrm{hi}}$. When high-fidelity costs differ materially
across items, $f$ must be the fraction of baseline service cost avoided, not a
simple fraction of candidate count.

An illustrative reading makes the ceiling concrete. With $f = 0.6$, a cheap
check at 5 percent of reference cost, and 10 percent overhead, perfect routing
yields $S_{\mathrm{review}} \approx 1.9$. Even if the preliminary check and
overhead were free, the zero-overhead ceiling would remain
$1/(1-f) = 2.5$ until validated coverage itself rises
(@fig-rejection-bound-ceiling).

```{python}
#| label: fig-rejection-bound-ceiling
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Validated coverage sets the upper bound.** In this service-cost model, a cheaper preliminary check moves its curve toward the ceiling, but only a check that correctly settles more cases can raise that ceiling. The curves are illustrative and use the displayed model with zero overhead; the argument depends on the saturation behavior rather than the exact values.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Line plot of review service-cost speedup versus validated preliminary-check coverage, with curve pairs for three preliminary-to-reference cost ratios saturating below an Amdahl-form ceiling set by coverage alone."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source: analytic curves from the chapter's displayed S_review model
# (no external data); values are illustrative, overhead epsilon = 0.
f_grid = [0.95 * i / 399 for i in range(400)]
ratios = [
    {"label": "cost ratio 0.01", "r": 0.01, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"label": "cost ratio 0.1", "r": 0.1, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"label": "cost ratio 0.3", "r": 0.3, "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

def s_review(cov, r, a):
    # S_review = 1 / ((1 - f) + a * (c_cheap / c_hi) + eps), with eps = 0
    return 1.0 / ((1.0 - cov) + a * r)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.25, 3.12))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.86, top=0.82, bottom=0.28)

ceiling = [1.0 / (1.0 - x) for x in f_grid]
ax.fill_between(f_grid, ceiling, 30, color=COLORS["grid"], alpha=0.35, zorder=0)
ax.text(0.04, 9.0, "unreachable without more validated coverage", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["muted"], fontstyle="italic")
ax.plot(f_grid, ceiling, color=COLORS["ink"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=2)

ax.axhline(1.0, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=1)
ax.text(0.93, 1.04, "no speedup", ha="right", va="bottom", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["muted"])

for c in ratios:
    ax.plot(f_grid, [s_review(x, c["r"], x) for x in f_grid], color=c["color"], linewidth=1.9, zorder=3, label=c["label"])
    ax.plot(f_grid, [s_review(x, c["r"], 1.0) for x in f_grid], color=c["color"], linewidth=1.2, linestyle=(0, (2, 2)), zorder=3)

ax.scatter([0.6], [2.5], marker="o", s=22, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=COLORS["ink"], linewidth=1.3, zorder=4)
ax.text(0.58, 2.95, "f = 0.6 caps the speedup at 2.5x", ha="right", va="bottom", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["ink"])

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(0, 0.95)
ax.set_ylim(0.7, 30)
ax.set_xlabel("validated coverage f  (share of cases settled without escalation)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("review service-cost speedup", fontsize=7)
ax.set_xticks([0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8])
ax.set_yticks([1, 2, 5, 10, 20])
ax.set_yticklabels(["1x", "2x", "5x", "10x", "20x"], fontsize=6.4)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2, labelsize=6.4)
ax.grid(axis="both", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=0)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

legend = ax.legend(
    loc="lower left",
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.0, 1.02),
    fontsize=6.0,
    frameon=True,
    facecolor="white",
    edgecolor="white",
    framealpha=0.92,
    handlelength=1.6,
    borderpad=0.15,
    labelspacing=0.3,
    ncol=1,
)
for text in legend.get_texts():
    text.set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Solid curves route the cheap check only where it settles the case (a = f); dashed color curves screen every candidate (a = 1). The black dashed line is the free-check ceiling 1/(1-f).",
    xywh=(0.10, 0.03, 0.80, 0.11),
    fontsize=5.5,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Within each cost ratio, the solid curve assumes perfect routing with $a=f$,
while the dashed curve charges the cheap check to every candidate with $a=1$.
Both remain below the same coverage ceiling, so lowering check cost cannot
compensate for low validated coverage.

The model holds review concurrency fixed because trusted review often scales
differently from generation. Reference checks may be scarce or licensed, and
named decision authority does not scale simply by adding reviewers. Different
reference flows cannot simply be pooled when they use different cases,
baselines, budgets, or downstream checks.

The model measures review service cost, not useful design progress.
Its $f$ counts only outcomes that agree with the declared high-fidelity
reference. Observed cheap-check coverage is not enough. Both cheap accepts and
cheap rejects need sampled escalation to measure false passes and false rejects,
and those audits belong in the cost. Independence reduces correlated
blind spots, but does not establish accuracy by itself.

Separating review cost from search quality matters. When proposals arrive
independently of the reviewer, faster generation cannot increase review
capacity. Adaptive search changes the proposal stream. A better generator can
reduce evaluations to a fixed-quality target without changing $f$. Search
quality, evaluations to target, and review service cost therefore need separate
measurements.

Estimating $f$ requires a fixed task distribution and generator, labels for
preliminary accepts and rejects, and random escalation from both groups to the
high-fidelity reference. The cost calculation must include routing, queueing,
audits, rework, and every case that still reaches the reference. Shared data,
models, objectives, or tool paths between the producer and preliminary check
must also be disclosed because they can create correlated blind spots.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{30\baselineskip}
```

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="Measuring review capacity"}
To estimate the model for a real AI-assisted study, define the measurement
procedure before the run.

- **Comparable unit:** State what one review case is, fix the task distribution,
  and use service-cost weighting when items are heterogeneous.
- **Outcome labels:** Record cheap accepts, cheap rejects, escalations, waivers,
  and the later reference outcome.
- **Matched audit:** Escalate random samples of both cheap accepts and cheap
  rejects; report false-pass and false-reject uncertainty.
- **Costs and routing:** Record $a$, $c_{\mathrm{cheap}}$,
  $c_{\mathrm{hi}}$, queueing, audits, and rework actually paid.
- **Independence:** Disclose shared actors, models, data, objectives, and tool
  paths between the producer and rejector.

Without these controls, $f$ is an assumption rather than a measurement.
:::

Holding the task and generator fixed while auditing both preliminary outcomes
allows architects to determine whether trusted review capacity is actually the
limiting resource.

When proposals arrive faster than trusted review can settle them, more
candidates only lengthen the queue. Improve throughput by validating cheaper
checks, reducing their cost, and cutting avoidable routing, audit, and
escalation overhead. For search, also measure whether a better generator
reaches the same quality with fewer evaluations.

The ISA-migration evaluation supplies an illustrative automation result, not a
measured point on this model. Its 30 percent repair rate comes from a selected
245-commit replay benchmark and combines generator behavior, benchmark
selection, and build/test coverage [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. The
paper did not report the matched audits and service costs needed to estimate
$f$. Estimating it would require holding the task distribution and generator
fixed, auditing both accepts and rejects at the reference fidelity, and
recording every service cost.

The arrival and service rates give a measurable form to a review-capacity gap,
but they are not a general law of search. Fast software work often has cheap
checks and low escalation overhead. RTL and physical-design work often has
costlier references and more cases that must escalate. That difference, rather
than raw candidate count alone, shifts useful AI work toward critique, evidence
review, and early rejection.

## What Transfers Across Operating Regimes

The task, architectural claim, run record, and named decision owner remain
necessary in all four regimes. What does not transfer automatically is the
validity of a workload assumption, baseline, proxy, failed-run interpretation,
or prior acceptance.

The regime changes when feedback latency, reversibility, consequence, interface
span, or check independence changes. The required evidence and decision rights
must change as well. Cheap feedback and local rollback can justify letting the
AI system execute more of the work. Expensive, irreversible, or system-wide
decisions make critique, evidence review, and independent challenge more useful
while named people retain authority for the decision.

A coding agent can often continue after a test suite passes because the patch
is inspectable and local rollback remains available. An architect cannot treat
a passing test as authorization when the change crosses an ABI, alters a
hardware interface, enters RTL, or supports a signoff decision. The task
structure and tool feedback may transfer, but the evidence burden and
permission to advance do not.

### Transfer Tests for a New Regime

Before reusing a method or artifact in another regime, ask three questions.

1. **Does the evidence still support the claim?** Recheck workload coverage,
tool and software versions, interfaces, objective definitions, and known
rejected regions. Parseable evidence can still be stale or out of scope.

2. **Can the cheaper check be trusted here?** Audit samples of both accepts and
rejects against the new reference path. Estimate false-pass and false-reject
behavior before using the check to avoid escalation.

3. **Did the AI role or decision rights change?** A move across an interface or
toward an irreversible decision may turn an action AI could execute into one it
may only recommend. It may also require different people to reject the result,
approve a waiver, recommend the change, or make the final decision even when
the method and artifact remain unchanged.

These tests catch two common failures. A single-layer improvement can violate
a system objective, and apparently current evidence can expire as the hardware
target, compiler, runtime, or workload distribution changes. Transfer means
revalidating the evidence and its scope, revising what the method may execute
or recommend, and reassigning decision rights where necessary rather than
copying prior results and approvals forward.

## Open Research Questions

The operating regimes show where evidence and decision rules are likely to fail
when an AI method moves across the computing stack. Five problems remain open.

1. **When can evidence from an AI-assisted study transfer between operating
regimes?** A workload result, proxy calibration, failed candidate, or accepted
baseline may lose its meaning when the software path, tool version, interface,
or cost of rollback changes. Research needs criteria for deciding which changes
require revalidation as the same artifact moves from software tuning through
simulation, implementation, and deployment.

2. **How can architects separate AI search quality from review capacity?** The
service-cost model holds the proposal stream fixed, while an adaptive generator
changes which candidates reach review. Quality reached per evaluation, service
cost per reviewed case, queueing, and preliminary-check errors must be
separated before faster progress can be attributed to better search or faster
review.

3. **How independent must a check be from the AI method it reviews?** Two
checks may use different tools while sharing training data, objective
functions, abstractions, or hidden assumptions. A useful measure of independence
would expose those dependencies and show whether a nominally separate check
catches failure classes missed by the producer.

4. **When does a local improvement found through AI-assisted search survive the
full system?** A compiler, runtime, accelerator, memory, network, or deployment
change can look favorable in isolation while worsening the end-to-end
objective. The evaluation must therefore connect the local result to the
complete software and hardware path under the same workload and decision
criterion.

5. **How long should evidence from an AI-assisted architecture study remain
valid?** Workloads, compilers, runtimes, tools, and process assumptions change
at different rates. A practical revalidation policy must catch stale evidence
before it supports a new decision without recreating every result after each
tool or workload change.

## Conclusion

Architects should use the operating regime to decide what an AI method may do
and what must be checked before the next architecture decision. Fast,
reversible work can allow more automated tuning under close tests. Model-mediated
exploration needs matched baselines, proxy calibration, and new evidence before
implementation.
Cross-interface and deployment work adds interface owners, guarded rollout,
telemetry limits, and rollback triggers. RTL, physical design, and signoff
shift useful AI work toward search narrowing, critique, bounded repair, and
evidence organization. Independent checks constrain the work, and named
reviewers and decision owners decide whether it advances.

In any of the four regimes, review can become the bottleneck. For a fixed stream
of comparable cases, cheaper preliminary checks reduce review cost only for the
cases they correctly settle. Audits, false passes, false rejects, routing, and
rework count toward that cost, and only greater validated coverage raises the
ceiling. Architects should first match AI work, evidence, checks, rollback, and
decision rights to the operating regime, then determine whether proposals or
trusted review are limiting progress.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** When the operating regime changes, revise what AI may do,
  the required evidence and rejection checks, the rollback plan, and the
  decision rights before advancing.
- **Reader test:** For the next transition, can you explain what AI may do, what
  can reject the result, how rollback works, and who may approve a waiver,
  recommend the change, or make the final decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-what-architect-owns examines what architects remain responsible for when work is delegated.
:::
